Vi-ho was the "well-known Dublin gentleman " upon 
whom it was proposed to confer the honorary .degree of 
LL.D. at the recent Senate of the Parochial University, 
and whose claim was rejected 1 The meeting of this pre-
cious Senate was private. It appears that the proceed-
ings at the private meeting were of a "lively" order. 
Though the Press was excluded, the Lord Chancellor said 
that the meeting was in no sense a confidential one. And 
so the Northern ·Whig, " through the kindness of a mem-
ber of the Senate," gave an account of the " lively " pro-
ceedings. In the course of the discussion which arose 
over the proposal to grant an LL.D. degree to a Belfast 
lady, Mr. E,. J. Gwyµn, it appea1·s, objected altogether 
to the principles on which he believed the degree was 
really proposed-namely, that something was done for a 
Dublin lady last year, and therefore something must be 
clone for a Belfast lady this year. 
He objected 1:n toto 
to this unhappy principle of balance being applied in 
Dublin University. 
People knew that there were those 
who thought th~t if in Ireland anything were done 
for party A then the same thing must be clone for party 
B. He objected altogether to this, and asked, " Where 
are you going to stop 1 Would any member of the Board 
say what w~s the real ground of the grace f' He desired 
to make this protest, but, having clone· so, did uot intend 
to oppose the grace. 
. The following, concerning some remarks by the old 
bigot of the Parochial, will be read with no surprise 
whatever·:-" The Provost said he had himself proposed 
the ~race to tho Boa1:c1, and took the full responsibility 
for 1t. 
He emphasised what the Senior Proctor had 
said, and went on to refer to the desirability of bringing 
~he University into touch with the Presbyterian element 
~n _the N~rth; but, no reporter having been present, it 
1s 1mposs1ble to state tho exad words he used. 
What-
ever they were, they gave rise to a somewhat heated dis-
cussion." 
The Provost evidently put his foot in it. 
Mr. 
· Russell, a Fellow, said that if he was to understand from 
what the Provost said that the degree was put forward 
on the ~r?und of expediency, or with the view of pleasing 
any rehg10us body as such, he would most certainly vote 
against it. 
Professor Dowden, who had risen at the 
same time as Mr. Russell, but had given way, identified 
himself entirely with what Mr. Russell had said, he, too, 
considering w11at the Provost had said to introduce an 
entirely inadmissable principle. 
Some otller·members of 
the Senate having spoken, the Vice-Chancellor put the 
question, and a considerable number of uou-placets hav-
ing been answered, Professor Culverwell :;;aid he did uot 
know whether he was in order in rising then; that he 
himself was not 
sure exactly what the Provost had 
meant, but he believed that the members who were vot-
ing uon-placet were doing so because they felt that they 
could give no sanction whatever to the view they believed 
the Provost to have put forward, and he appealed to the -
Board to put the degree on the ground of its being one 
given to a representative of higher education in Ireland. 
Subsequently, the bigot Provost, after some delay, rose 
and said that he had not originally, or at any ,time, put. 
forward the degree 011 the ground objected to, which he 
had only spoken of as an incidental advantage, and it 
was certainly ' one to which he attached importance, 
though he did not urge it as justifying the degree. 
One of the proposed graces was withdrawn by the. 
Board. 
In the course of some general remarks Professor 
Culverwell said " that there was a, very general feeling 
that the ~ .Jard, in selecting names for submission to the 
Senate, Lael not lJeeu mindful of the high traditions of 
the past, and he made au eloquent appeal to them and to 
the Senate to remember that an honorary degree was the 
highest distinction the University could bestow, and that 
it would be impossible to over-estimate the ca.re and dis-
crimination which should be applied in selecting the 
names-." It was evidently feared by several who ought 
to b~ in a position to judge tliat poor Trinity of the Medi-
cal School, poor Trinity whose gates are passed by 'so 
many of the suobocracy whose parents can afford to send 
them to Oxford and Cambridge, Trinity on whose behalf 
Sir John Nutting attempted to do some recruiting work 
by proffering bribes to poor Papists-it was evidently 
feared that declassed Trinity was about to develop a 
policy of " dealing," to some extent, it: its honornry 
degrees.

The late Recorder of Dublin, Sir Frederick Falkiner 
made his parting bow at Green Street Courthouse 01; 
Saturday last. 
The fact is of little or no interest to us. 
There is a certain lrnlo hovering round venerable rm:-
me,is, that one hesitates to lay a heavy hand upon it. 
According to the report in p,;111,:, Sir Frederick Falkiner 
said : -" To the great Press of the capital I owe a deep 
debt of gratitude which I would now most cordially ex-
press, for the more tlrn.n kindness and forbearance, the 
more than justice which they have accorded to me, and 
which has often powerfully supported me on occasions of 
difficulties." 
Certainly "the great Press of the capital" 
is a brilliant stroke of unconscious sarcasm. 
Pink, 
Green, B1tf/', Alf Fox ; oh, what a brilliant Press! 
To-
wards the end of Sir Frederick Falkiner's remarks he 
said-" Farewell'!" 
He went on:-" I sa,y it now to 
all-that your fare through life ma.y be well, indeed; 
that welfare may be yours in all your paths through life, 
whatever they may be, both public, professional, and 
private ; and I .say it accompanying it wit,h its other old 
English equivalent-Good-bye-in its ancient and ori-
ginal meaning o'f ' God be with ye.' '' The " old Eng-
lish equivalent " is entertaining. 
"Why not au old Irish 
equivalent 1 Had the Recorder been up to date like the 
mashers who parade at the Dalkey band, he would have 
used the new E:nglish equivalent, a.nd would have s,tid-
" Ta, ta, old chappies." Being an old man who, for 
many years, enjoyed a goocl, fat job, he said "Good-
bye." 'When will we have an Irish Recorder who will 
. leave his office witb a "be,v1n <l ct: 'De l1t'> '' on his lips!

[CONFIDENTIAL.]

One Thomas

The Shareholders’ Committee of the Great S. and W.
Railway have issued a report. In the course of some
preparatory remarks over the names of Messrs. M. Sul-
livan and J. O’Donohoe, we read:—

You will observe from its perusal that the grounds upon which
your proxies were solicited in the circular letter of Sir William
Goulding, dated May 9th, were tacitly or expressly abandoned
at tho meeting. Tt was suggcested to you in that circular that the
signatories of the requisition represented merely an insignificant
fraction, little more than one per-cent., of the total original Stock
of the Company. The requisition, of course, merely conformed to
the law, and the list of Stockholders who have approved our pro-
posal is the adequate reply to the misleading suggestion. Tho
charge was made that the movement in favour of an Inquiry
into the affairs of the Company was “a factious agitation, not
calculated to serve the interests of the Company.” That pretence
was abandoned by the Chairman at the Meeting, and a quasi
apology tendered therefor, while the movement of our Stock in
the Money Market since the Shareholders began to show an interest
| in the management of the Company, so far from being adverse,
has been in the direction of improvement. Not less misleading
was the innuendo in the Chairman’s circular that this movement
has been inspired by some obscure “religious element.”  The
references to that subject at the Meeting will, it is to be hoped.
prevent such innuendos from being used again. Another sugges
tion of the Chairman, which was not maintained, and could not
be maintained, in the public Meeting of the Stockholders, was
that the propcsal of our Committee was “to give an unnamed
and irresponsible Committee unlimited powers to sanction endless
expenditure by the appointment of experts and an additional staft.”
The naming of the Committee was a matter for the Meeting in
consultation with the Chairman and Directors. The description of
such a Committee as ““irresponsible” was, of course,.absurd, for it
[ would have the same responsibility to the Stockholders as the
[ % Board itself, and its recommendations could be adopted only in
I virtue of the Stockholders’ approval and endorsement, while the
prespect of “endless expenditure by the appointment of experts
and an additional staff ” was purely imaginary, and did not with-
stand a moment’s discussion at the public Mecting. The parallel

fortunate enterprises, the Midland Great Western Railway Com-

One of the most important factors in the fight for re-

DEAR Sm,-Put the inclosed money order to my 
credit in renewal of my subscription for the current 
year, and have the kindness likewise to make me an 
acknowledgement. 
I rarely have time to write even a brief note, but I 
avail willingly of the present occasion to make .time so 
as to tell you of the satisfaction which a perusal of TUE 
LEADER gi:ves me, and has given me, since a good friend 
and fellow-countryman called my attention to the pub-
lication .some three years ago. It is refreshing, , enter-
taining and -instructive. 
There is real moral courage 
shown in its columns-a commodity of which there ap-
pears to be a rather scant supply in Ireland these many 
,years, I regret to say, 
Moral courage is greater than 
physical courage. 
Great moral courage bes11eaks the 
higher type of 1!1-an ; great physical courage the higher 
ty1je of animal. In that sense, every one is au animal 
-at least when brought to bay; but very few are men. 
You have shown great moral courage in assailing 
many phases of the unnatural life that has existed in 
Ireland time out of mind, and which no newspaper in 
Ireland of note-no newspaper at all, in fact-has ever 
before dared tackle; but the greatest service of all in 
this line, in my judgment, was the vigorous assaults 
which you have made again and again, and made suc-
cessfully, on tho "Shoueen" colleges and convents, 
who ha.ve been permitted unhindered for generations 
to turn loose upon the country piping pocket editions 
of Johnny Bull, instea.d of stalwart and patriotic young 
Irish men and women, proud of their country and its 
achievements. 
If you had done no more than this you 
would have deserved well of your clay and generation. 
The hr.ave l<'enians put life into the country in their 
day, and pl'oved the greatest driving force in the poli-
tics of Ireland during the past century. 
The reason 
was the Fenians knew what they were after and made 
preparations to go for it. 
THE ·LEADER, with scheme 
scarcely less comprehensive, but employing different 
methods, knows also what it is after and is bravely 
reaching out for it. 
And you seem to make a bull's 
eye every time. 
The insulting treatment the editor of Tim LEADER re-
ceived in the Language Parade should be characterized 
as it deserves. 
" Incident " is too mild a term I think; 
"outrage " would better :fit what happened. 
A man 
who hasn't enemies isn't worth his salt. As an Ameri-
can Irishman, I find it hard to understand how one who 
has rendered such signa.I service to Ireland should have 
been turned out of a public function in the capital 
city of the country-because you wrote some things that 
were offensive to some people. 
I myself also find fault 
with the manner as well as the matter of some of the 
things you write. I feel it wrong and. un~1.1st that you 
should refer to the physical force pa'i:ty, for instanc~, 
as " Tin Pikemen. " 
Those who follow that form of 
fait-h (in which I am proud to aver, I am a :firm be-
liever), are in the main, I feel certain, brave, honest 
n ncl unselfish; and their spirit it was· that blew tµ.e 
breath of life again on Ireland when she was "a corpse 
on the dissecting table." 
When all is said, however, that can be said and that 
ought be said in favoul' of the physical force men, the 
fact remains that the treatment of the editor of THE 
LEADER in that Language Parade, and his host Dr. 
McWalter, was tyranny. 
The outrage gives a chance 
to those opposed to Ireland to say that the minority 
in that country would be crushed were the maj_~rity 
permitted to have thefr way. 
Our people--or some 
among them a.nyhow-do not seem much attuned as 
yet to self control in political controversy. 
They want 
to knock down a man who differs from them. • An in-
vited guest on an occasion like that deserves special 
attention and protection, and .would be entitled to both, 
according to my notion of things, even if he had 
thought fit to invite to a seat in his carriage the Grand 
Ma.ster of the Orangemeu himself! 
If the Committee that issued_ the invitation felt they 
were entitled to s~y 
who. Dr. 
McWalter should 
choose to bring along with him, they also had the 
l'ight to ins'i'st on the colour of the Doctor's clothes, the 
shape of his hat, the cut of his trousers, the texture of 
his socks; and, finally, that it should be a mare and 
not a gelding that drew the carriage. In fact, tJ,ie 
Doctor was no longer a free agent; for the C'ommittee 
must have felt that it owned him body and soul and 
even mind that clay! 
Tho affair altogether seems even at this distance to 
have boen inhospitable and un-Irish; and it is greatly 
to the credit of the array of priests and representative 
laymen throughout the country who have written you 
to express the auger they felt at what happened-a 
chorus of condemnat,ion in which I myself, au utter 
stranger to you, feel impelled to joiu.-Very truly yours, 
J. P. HENRY, M.D. 
[We would like to point out to Dr. J. P. Henry, of 
New York, 
that the term "Tin Pikemen" 
is not 
aimed at rebels, but at lip-rebels-two different things.

SCHOLARS AND SCHOLARSHIPS.

T have been moving fast in the matter of Univer-
sity education. Not that we are any ncarer a
Catholic Univeisity. But it is a very hopeful sign that
men are at long last beginning to take an interest in
higher education as apart from the Higher Education
Question. It is coming to be recognised that in the
struggle between Irish Catholics and the Irish Govern-
ment bhero is a third party concerned—to wit, the Irish
Catholic student, and that the interests of this unfortu-
nate have been sadly neglected during the fifty years
war. Hence we aro at last all beginning to see that be
the solution satisfactory or unsatisfactory, bc_lb a solu-
tion or be it only an expedient, we must provide educa-
tion somehow in the meantime, and the long-neglected
Catholic student has a right to call on his countrymen
to assist him in that regard.

The recent House of Commons debate has had this ex-
cellent result, that, as far as Parliament is concerned,
it has shown the hopelessness of our cause. The failure
of Parliamentary agitation has, in this matter, been
hopeless and complete. ~We have, therefore, been
thrown back upon our own resources. Now when we
come to reckon these, one fact shines out with abundant
clearness, that there are only two places in Ireland
where a man can get a degree, and that it is with a
view to getting a degree that most men pursue their
studies. We are thus faced with the necessity of
utilising either Trinity or the Royal University, and the
views of Dr. Walter M‘Donald or Father Finlay become
the only possible alternatives.  Dr. Walter M‘Donald
urges the capture of Trinity ; Father Finlay would have
us develop our own resources by making use of the not
very perfect Royal University.

Now, at first sight, the capture of Trinity seems a very
feasible and a somewhat attractive scheme. Four hun-
dred enemies would matriculate within its gates, pledged
to Irish Ireland and its destruction. A solid band they
would keep their own ideas, fight for the Faith, sweep the
examinations, and finally force a capitulation by winning
its fellowships. ~Young Catholics attend German Uni-
versities, where the staff is practically as Protestant as
that of T.C.D., and, as Dr. M‘Donald points out, they
also attend Paris University, where it is probably even
more anti-Catholic. Now in all this there is a great mea-
sure of truth, and yet I maintain the scheme to be in-
feasible. Let me state my reasons:—

Firstly, it is not because I believe that these young
men would be likely to become agnostics. As far as my
experience of Trinity College Catholics at present goes,
they do not—to judge from outward signs—give up their
religion. They are mostly frequent communicants, and
seem to pray fervently in Church. Judging from per-
sonal experience, I should say that a Catholic going to
T.C.D. comes out a good Catholic, but a bad Irishman; I
know, indeed, one, or possibly two, bad Catholics among
Trinity men; I also know one, or possibly two, good
Irishmen. Perhaps, indeed, a T.C.D. man remains a
good Catholic for the same reason that a cynic would
account for tho strict morality of some atheists;
they can’t afford to be otherwise; they know that
everyone is watching them. But he gives up his na-
tionality as a compensation. Now if the four hundred
crusaders entored, I believe this state of things would
not, for the present, be very greatly changed, though a
larger number would be somewhat more liable to agnos-
tical and less liable to anti-Irish influences.

A BALM-BRINGING BREEZE.*

G HE Society for the Preservation of the Irish Lan-

. guage have just put on the market a volume of

great interest: Leoizne Anoeap, “A Southern Breeze,”

is the name given to a volume of selected original poetry

by Mrv. Tadhg O’Donoghue. An introduction giving a

metrical analysis of the poems is prefixed. Explanatory

notes and a very full and elaborate vocabulary are

added. Thus even the most elementary student can

scarcely be baulked inhis endeavour to extract the

poetic sweets from Mr. O’Donoghue’s verse. This pains-

taking and praiseworthy catering for the student of rudi-

mentary Irish suggests sad reflections. It points out

with unmistakable clearness how far removed we are

from a revival of the Irish language such as would give

the poet or prose-writer that immunity from glossarial

and grammatical drudgery as regards his own composi-

tions which is so necessary to the free exercise of literary

genius. It is, indeed, difficult to drink deep of the

Pierian Spring if the poet is all the time conscious that
he must explain his metric system, parse his intri-

cate and subtle phrases, gloss his more difficult

words, and do all this in a language radically different

from the language of his poetical inspiration, before

he can hope to reach an audience of appreciable extent,
and that this audience once reached is, for the most part,
in a state of open hostility to his thought, his spirit, his

metaphor, his language. A poet has the privilege of veil-
ing the imperfect conception of his thought in a mist of

obscure language. The reader half-understands, half-
admires, the clouded word-drapery, the metaphors
struggling into light, the muffled similes. But if the
unhappy author has to furnish the key to his poetic

sleight-of-hand, if he has to reduce the divine utterances
of the muse to the chilling rules of grammar, if he has to

coop his sublimest thoughts within the limits of a phrase
in a foreign tongue, the enchantment of true poetry is
apt to vanish in the manipulation of the literary labo-
ratory. These arc some of the difficulties that every
modern Irish poet has to encounter. The volume be-
“fore me proves that Mr. O’Donoghuc has wrestled

with these difficulties with no inconsiderable
success. This hook proves him to be the legi-
timate descendant of the great family of

Munster poets whose genealogy goes back to pre-his-
toric ages, and whose race never became extinct, though
it had few living representatives in the concluding de-
cades of the last century. To the orchestral tempest
that swept through the Irish lyre during the seven-
teenth and ecighteenth centuries when Seafradh and
Aodhaghan and Eoghan poured forth their souls in un-
premeditated song—to this rhythmical tempest there
succeeded a calm so still, so peaceful, that many de-

clared it the breathlessness of death. Sincere lovers
of the Irish muse were content with admiring “the
mild, angelic air, the rapture of repose’ of a mori-
bund poetry : —

“ Before decay’s effacing fingers
Had swept the lines where beauty lingers;’”

“They steadfastly gazed on the face of the dead,
And bitterly thought of the morrow,””

the sad morrow when Irish poetry would no longer be
alive.  Suddenly a “cooling southern breeze’’ “whispers
through the trees,”” and “brings balm to the brow,’” and
thrills the soul with the hope of an era of regeneration
in Irish poetry. ~ With the advent of this poetic
bantling let us fondly hope that a new era in Irish
literature will commence; as the birth of a favoured
child was expected to herald a new age of gold in the
famous Eclogue of Virgil. JIn the poetic world let us
hope we can say with truth:—

“ Magnus ab integro sxclorum nascitur ordo;
Tam redit et Virgo, redeunt Saturnia regna;
Tam nova progenies coelo demittitur alto.”

The new heaven and the new ecarth of the Helico-
nian realms, which we fain would see created, must,
indeed, preserve the spirit of the old and much of the
form; but there must be a distinct development—a
distinct effort to catch up the voices of the living pre-
sent, and to give them a new existence in the sym-
phony of song. In poetry, as in other matters, it is
the business of the ‘“dead past’ to “bury its dead.”
If in our poetry of the future we have to chant an
elegy for the great ones that are no more, if we have to
take our stand on ground hallowed by the brave deeds
of martyrs and of heroes, if we tune our verse to sad-
ness as we sing

“Of old, unhappy, far-off things
And battles long ago ”’

let not the plaintiveness of the note we strike prevent
us from breathing into that verse the spirit of the liv-
ing present. Even while we sound the depths of sorrow
let us never fail to bathe our souls in the airiness of
spirit, the gaiety, the sunuy mirthfulness, the imper-
turbable humour of our ancient and imperishable race.
We must try to catch the spirit of our people in its
present state of growth and development. We must
not be unmindful of the dignity that belongs to the
present era of our national life in which we can look
back with conscious triumph on ““ages of bondage and
slaughter ”’ through which we suffered and fought for
“virtue and Erin,”” and won the victory over ‘the
Saxon and guilt ’’ with a decisiveness that has engaged
the attention of the world, and is destined to “re-
sound through its history.”” The representative Irish
poet of the dawning era of a revived literature should
endeavour to saturate his verse, whatever be the sub-
ject, with the spivit of a revived and regenerated
people.  He must give expression to the insatiable
hunger for greatness, to the unquenchable ambition for
what is noble and enduring in human life, which are
characteristic of the historic Irish race. Our coming
poetry—if it be truly Irish—will reveal its genuineness
by sounding the note of national progress and national
regeneration, by dealing with lofty subjects, by the
dignity of its tone, by its matchless melody, by display-
ing in every verse the effects of a deep infusion of the
joyousness of spirit, the strength, the subtlety of the
Gael. In our new poefry, let us aim at more elevated
subjects than our ancestors sang of : —

“Paulo majora canamus.
Non omnes arbusta juvant humilesque myricz ;
Si canimus slyvas, slyve sint consule digne.’’

A LIMERICK BAZAAR.

T.HE colunns of the Leaprr have, from time to time,

. been filled with descriptions of entertainments
held in support of various interests not essentially con-
nected with Trish Ireland. Now and again it has been
deemed desirable to devote some space to remarks.on func-
tlops, with the aims of whose promoters noue bub
Britons of the most western type could have sympathy,
in order to draw attention to the support that has been
accorded thereto by the gemeral public. The recent
Military Tournament is an instance in point. Agoin,
enterprises such as bazaars have claimed consideration
at intervals since they represent a phase of national
existence, and since it behoves us to study ourselves at
play quite as much as when employed in serious occu-
pations. This being so, and since to raise money for a
hospital is to engage in a good work, we may, with pro-
priety, discuss Garryowen IT. B

Garryowen II. is not a race-horse. Neither is it a
yacht.  King Edward VII. and Sir Thomas Lipton
were in no way identified with it. It was nothing more,
or less, than a bazaar held in Limerick last week to assist
in wiping off the debt on Harrington’s Hospital. In-
asmuch as a ‘bazaar yclept Garryowen had been held for
a similar purpose ten years ago, it was considered good
to call the féte of 1905 Garryowen II. It was, as the
Mayor observed at the opening ceremony, a charity that
appealed to “all creeds and classes.”” "Yet I believe I
am not wrong in stating that those whose interest pre-
dominates in the Hospital affect the simpler Chuis-
tianity, and that being so it is soothing to persons of
less strenuous qualities to find them twice in a decade
appealing for financial aid.

The word ““ bazaar ’’ suggests the gorgeous East. At
least, it did at one time, but it may at present, with
truth, be said to give rise to less Oriental ideas. Were it
still the case that, at shows of this description, an
effort was made to reproduce the markets of Cairo, then
there would be no reason to wonder at the sameness
that pervades them all. Since, however, the Eastern
model has long been abandoned, it is surely strange
that “local colour ’’ is so conspicuously absent. A few
weeks ago I dealt in these columns with a bazaar at
Ballsbridge. Much that I wrote of that endeavour to
extract money from the public may be applied with
equal propriety to the recent enterprise in Limerick.
The latter was, indeed—to amend a familiar phrase—
the same only less so. It was on a smaller scale, of
course. It was local rather than national. But it had
merry-go-rounds, a switch-back calculated to stir even
deeper emotions than were appealed to by the charit-
able object on behalf of which it ran its zig-zag course,
cinematographs, bands, and the inevitable cafe chantant.
It differed from its precursor in Dublin in soliciting
help through the medium of raflle-tickets, in permitting
games of chance, and, further, in possessing a bar.
There was no abuse of any of these sources of revenue,
and if the All-Treland Temperance Fete showed how
successfully a bazaar might be conducted without these
aids, that in Limerick was an example of the fact that
they are not necessarily clements of disaster.

TIn Jocal colour, however, the bazaar was singularly
lacking. At the recent show in Ballsbridge there were
Trish concerts, but not so in Limerick. The cafe w_lu_ch
was nightly thronged suggested rather the British
music-hall than the Boulevards of Paris. It certainly
did not awaken memories of Garryowen’s Garden. A
Colleen Bawn warbling by the spacious Shannon that
“ there was a girl wanted there,”” was a disillusion. So
were artistes from London in the latest comics. Save
for the soft doric of the men and women, an}l a scene
depicting King John’s Castle, the bazaar might have

THE LEADER.

Jury 1, 1905

been one organised in Surbiton to remove the debt on
the local Parochial Hall.

A Military Tournament on two occasions added zest
to the afternoon programme. But for this item, how,
ever, the bazaar, if not marked by any characteristically
Irish features, was at least free from pronouncedly
British displays. That emblem of Liberty—The Union
Jack—did, indeed, hang limply from a flag-post in the
grounds, but it was not aggressive, not blatant. The
stalls were, as is customary, stocked with everything
that nobody required. Why anyone should imagine
that the bazaar-frequenter is likely to be filled with a
desire to buy potatoes or hats, or will be unable to
restrain his yearning for a pound of butter, is one of
those conundrums that in the hot weather it is wiser
not to attempt to solve. And the heat during Garry-
owen II. was great and wonderfud, none the less the
dancing halls were thronged, the swing-boats were filled,
and the tropical climate in no way restrained the good
citizens of Limerick from the more violent forms of exer-
cise whereby it was sought at once to afford them plea-
sure and to deprive them of cash.

‘We have the authority of the bard for the statement
that Limerick is beautiful. It seems a pity, therefore,
that prettier grounds than those of the Market field
could not be secured for such fetes as Garryowen II.
Perhaps a stranger unacquainted with local conditions
has no right to criticise on that point. The want, how-
ever, of distinctive local features, the absolute cosmo-
politanism of the whole affair is surely a matter of re-
gret,although as things are now it may not be easy to
indicate how an improvement in this direction may be
effected.

Garryowen II. had, however, one feature peculiarly
Limerick—everyone apologised for it.

Trinity College—namely, Trinity itself. We have re-
cently seen placed before the youth of Ireland by the
Board of Trinity College the offer of valuable Entrance
Scholarships to be awarded to them as the result of the
Intermediate Examinations, without further test. This
offer enables us to measure with considerable accuracy
the standards of Trinity College, compared with those of
the Royal University. These New Scholarships, be it
noted, are offered not only to Exhibitioners of the Senior
Grade, but also to those of the Middle Grade; that is,
to boys of sixteen, and not unfrequently of fifteen years
of age, who have not yet completed the ordinary school
course. We are bound to presume that the Board of
Trinity College, in offering such inducements to those
brilliant boys of that age to enter Trinity College, con-
sider that they are already fitted to follow with due
profit the courses of the University. If they are not
thus qualified—that is, if the standard of Honours in
the Junior Freshman year is notably higher than that of
the Senior Grade to which they would have passed if
they remained at school—then the Board of Trinity Col-
lege, in inducing them to enter that Institution, will
have seriously injured the educational career of these
young students. In the Royal University, it has been
found by experience that students have little or no
chance of distinguishing themselves at Matriculation in
